title: Fundamental types and initialization

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ types determine representation, valid operations, initialization, lifetime, overload selection, and value category behavior, with cv and reference qualifiers refining access.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: auto count = values.size() deduces the container's unsigned size type rather than an assumed int. Your goal is to declare initialized Boolean, character, integer and floating objects without unjustified size assumptions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Program structure and main; pf state expressions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ types determine representation, valid operations, initialization, lifetime, overload selection, and value category behavior, with cv and reference qualifiers refining access. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Fundamental widths vary, auto follows deduction rules rather than runtime types, references are not reseatable objects, and class invariants add semantic constraints. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: auto makes a variable dynamically typed so its type can change after later assignments.

includes: Choose types and initialize objects using syntax that exposes narrowing.
Declare initialized Boolean, character, integer and floating objects without unjustified size assumptions.
excludes: Fundamental widths vary, auto follows deduction rules rather than runtime types, references are not reseatable objects, and class invariants add semantic constraints.

outcomes: Declare initialized Boolean, character, integer and floating objects without unjustified size assumptions.

examples.title: Fundamental types and initialization: defined case
examples.situation: auto count = values.size() deduces the container's unsigned size type rather than an assumed int.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: auto count = values.size() deduces the container's unsigned size type rather than an assumed int.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: auto performs compile-time type deduction once; the variable then has that fixed deduced type for its lifetime.
examples.result: The defined result follows because auto performs compile-time type deduction once; the variable then has that fixed deduced type for its lifetime.

examples.title: Fundamental types and initialization: boundary case
examples.situation: const std::string& binds to an existing string for read-only access without copying under its lifetime rules.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: const std::string& binds to an existing string for read-only access without copying under its lifetime rules.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Fundamental widths vary, auto follows deduction rules rather than runtime types, references are not reseatable objects, and class invariants add semantic constraints.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: auto makes a variable dynamically typed so its type can change after later assignments.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Declare initialized Boolean, character, integer and floating objects without unjustified size assumptions.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: auto makes a variable dynamically typed so its type can change after later assignments.
misconceptions.correction: auto performs compile-time type deduction once; the variable then has that fixed deduced type for its lifetime.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: auto makes a variable dynamically typed so its type can change after later assignments.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Fundamental types and initialization: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Fundamental widths vary, auto follows deduction rules rather than runtime types, references are not reseatable objects, and class invariants add semantic constraints.

recap.summary: You should now be able to declare initialized Boolean, character, integer and floating objects without unjustified size assumptions. Keep this boundary in view: Fundamental widths vary, auto follows deduction rules rather than runtime types, references are not reseatable objects, and class invariants add semantic constraints.
